Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُقَدِّمُوا۟ بَيْنَ يَدَىِ ٱللَّهِ وَرَسُولِهِۦ ۖ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ (۱)
O you who have faith! Do not venture ahead of Allah and His Apostle, and be wary of Allah. Indeed Allah is all-hearing, all-knowing. (1)

Key Points

| Three Surahs—Al-Ma'idah, Al-Hujurat, and Al-Mumtahanah—which deal with governmental and social issues, begin with the phrase, «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا» (O you who have believed). In the beginning of Surah Al-Ma'idah, we read: «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا اوفوا بالعقود» (O you who have believed! Fulfill [your] covenants). In Surah Al-Mumtahanah, we read: «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا لاتتّخذوا عدوّى و عدوّكم اولیاء» (O you who have believed! Do not take My enemy and your enemy as allies). And in this Surah, we read: O you who have believed! Do not precede the judgment of Allah and His Messenger. This Ayah seeks to train the believers to be like the angels, for the Quran says about them: They do not precede Him in speech and they act only by His command. «لایَسبِقونَه بِالقَول و هم باَمرِه یَعمَلون»(1) The Quran has not specified the instances of preceding or getting ahead of the Prophet, so that the prohibition includes all types of preceding in creedal, social, political, economic, and other matters, whether in word or deed. Allah has strictly forbidden preceding the leader of the Islamic society, because whoever precedes Allah and the Prophet in his affairs causes disruption in the management of the Islamic system, leads the society toward chaos, and, in reality, treats the legislative system as a plaything of his own desires. Acting upon this Ayah prevents many errors. Sometimes, the demands of the people, following human speculation and conjecture, the desire for innovation, hasty judgment, or the assumption of free-thinking compels a person to say, write, or make decisions that unintentionally precede the will of Allah and His Messenger. Just as a group, under the guise of worship, decisiveness, revolutionary spirit, or asceticism and simple living, preceded Allah and the Messenger, and, as the saying goes, became "hotter than the soup." Accepting and acting upon social manners, customs, and traditions, or human regulations and laws that do not have their roots in the Quran and Hadith, and do not stem from intellect and innate nature (fitrah), is a form of preceding Allah and the Messenger. Examples of Preceding Let us pay attention to a few historical examples of preceding the Prophet that have been mentioned in the commentaries and narrations: 1. On Eid al-Adha, a group sacrificed before the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله. They were told: «لاتقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله»(2) 2. The Prophet of Islam صلى الله علیه وآله sent a group to preach to the disbelievers. The disbelievers killed the Prophet's representatives, and only three people managed to escape. On their way back, these three killed two disbelievers from the Bani 'Amir tribe in revenge for the killing of their friends, even though those two were innocent. The Quran rebuked them for this arbitrary action, asking why they committed this act without the Prophet's command?(3) «لاتقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله» 3. The tribe of Bani Tamim requested an Amir (commander) and ruler from the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله. The first and second Caliphs each proposed a person and argued with each other, claiming their candidate was better. The Ayah was revealed: «لاتقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه... و لاترفعوا اصواتكم»(4) 4. An infallible Imam عليه السلام told a person to recite this supplication: «لااله الاّ اللّه...» up to the point where it says: «یُحیى و یُمیت» (He gives life and causes death). The listener added a phrase on his own and said: «و یمیت و یحیى» (and causes death and gives life). The Imam said: Your phrase is correct, but say what I tell you, and then recited the Ayah «لاتقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله» for him.(5) 5. Some companions of the Prophet of Islam صلى الله علیه وآله forbade themselves sleep, food, and marital relations. The Prophet became upset, gathered the people, and said: I myself eat, sleep, and live with my wife. This is my way and my life's conduct, so whoever turns away from this path is not of me. «فمَن رَغِب عن سنّتى فلیس منّى»(6) 6. Although the Prophet of Islam صلى الله علیه وآله declared temporary marriage (Mut'ah) lawful and legal, the second Caliph forbade it. This itself is a type of preceding the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله, which is forbidden in this Ayah. «لاتقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله» 7. In the eighth year of the Hijra, when the Prophet of Islam صلى الله علیه وآله departed from Medina for the Conquest of Mecca, some Muslims did not break their fast during this journey, even though they knew a traveler is not required to fast and they saw that the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله had broken his fast. These people were, in fact, preceding the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله.

1) Al-Anbiya, 27. 2) Tafsir Kashshaf. 3) Tafsir Kashshaf. 4) Sahih Bukhari, Vol. 3, p. 123. 5) Khisal Saduq, Vol. 2, p. 62. 6) Wasail, Vol. 23, p. 244.

Messages

  1. Before requesting or commanding an action, the psychological grounds for acceptance must first be created in the addressee. The phrase «یا ایها الّذین آمنوا» grants personality to the believers and expresses their relationship with Allah, which is the basis for carrying out the command.

  2. Since the command not to precede Allah and the Messenger is a command of etiquette, this Ayah itself addresses the audience with special courtesy. «یا ایها الّذین آمنوا...»

  3. Declaring lawful what Allah has made unlawful, or declaring unlawful what He has made lawful, is a type of preceding Allah and the Messenger. «لاتقدّموا...»

  4. Any form of innovation (bid'ah) or legislation in opposition to Divine law is forbidden. «لا تقدّموا...»

  5. The source of our law and conduct must be the Quran and the Sunnah of the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله. «لا تقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله»

  6. The ruling of the Messenger is the ruling of Allah, and disrespect toward him is disrespect toward Allah. «لا تقدّموا بین یدى اللَّه و رسوله»

  7. Preceding Allah and the Messenger is a lack of piety. «لاتقدّموا... واتّقوا»

  8. For carrying out duties, two levers—faith (Iman) and piety (Taqwa)—are necessary. «آمنوا... واتّقوا»

  9. Practical commitment must be accompanied by inner piety. «لاتقدّموا... واتّقوا اللَّه»

  10. Those who precede Allah and His Messenger due to personal tastes or social customs have distanced themselves from faith and piety. «لاتقدّموا... و اتّقوا»

  11. We should not justify our excesses. «لا تقدّموا... انّ اللَّه سمیع علیم»

  12. Belief in Allah's presence and awareness becomes the basis for abstinence and fear of God. «و اتّقوا اللّه انّ اللَّه سمیع علیم»

Explanations

| Portrait of Surah Al-Hujurat This Surah was revealed in Medina and contains eighteen Ayahs. "Hujurat" is the plural of "Hujrah" (chamber), and since the fourth Ayah of this Surah speaks about the chambers of the house of the Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله, the Surah has been named Al-Hujurat. In this Surah, which is also known as the Surah of Manners (Adab), important ethical and social topics are discussed, some of which are mentioned below: 1. The manners of interacting with the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله and not preceding him. 2. Avoiding mockery, giving bad nicknames, suspicion (su' al-zann), spying (tajasus), and backbiting (ghibah). 3. Establishing social relations based on brotherhood (ukhuwwah), reconciliation (islah dhat al-bayn), general mobilization against rebels, just mediation, and avoiding rumor-mongering. 4. Valuing individuals in society based on their degrees of faith and piety, and avoiding disbelief (kufr), transgression (fisq), and sin. 5. Following the Divine Prophet out of love and awareness, and avoiding any form of boasting (mannat) due to accepting Islam and acting upon its commands. In this short Surah, the address «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا» is repeated five times, which indicates the high status of ethical issues and social relations in Islam, and clearly shows that the manner in which Muslims interact with one another and with their leaders has been a concern of the Holy Lawgiver.

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تَرْفَعُوٓا۟ أَصْوَٰتَكُمْ فَوْقَ صَوْتِ ٱلنَّبِىِّ وَلَا تَجْهَرُوا۟ لَهُۥ بِٱلْقَوْلِ كَجَهْرِ بَعْضِكُمْ لِبَعْضٍ أَن تَحْبَطَ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ وَأَنتُمْ لَا تَشْعُرُونَ (۲)
O you who have faith! Do not raise your voices above the voice of the Prophet, and do not speak aloud to him like you shout to one another, lest your works should fail without your being aware. (2)

Key Points

In the previous Ayah, exceeding the Messenger of Allah and divine commands in action was prohibited, and in this Ayah, raising the voice in the presence of the Prophet during speech and conversation is prohibited. In Ayah 63 of Surah An-Noor, it is also advised not to call the Prophet like you call one another, but rather to address Him respectfully and courteously.

Preserving the deed is more important than the deed itself. Our deeds are sometimes flawed from the start because they begin with the intention of showing off (Riya) and ostentation; sometimes they are destroyed midway due to vanity and arrogance; and sometimes they are nullified (habṭ) at the end due to certain actions. For this reason, the Quran states: Whoever brings his deed until the Day of Judgment and insures it against afflictions will be rewarded tenfold: «مَن جاء بالحسنة فله عشر امثالها»(7) and it does not say: Whoever performs a deed will have a tenfold reward. This is because there is a great distance between performing the deed in this world and delivering it on the Day of Judgment. As the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said in a narration: For every remembrance (dhikr), a tree is planted in Paradise. A person said: Then we will have many trees in Paradise! The Prophet replied: Yes, but sometimes sins and actions occur from you that burn those trees. Then He recited the Ayah above.(8)

The Quran mentions the nullification of deeds (habṭ al-a'māl) in one place for disbelief (kufr) and polytheism (shirk), and in another place for disrespect in the presence of the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله. Thus, the punishment for disrespecting the Prophet is equivalent to the punishment for disbelief and polytheism.

One of the Prophet's companions who had spoken loudly in the Prophet's presence became very distressed when he heard that his loud voice would cause his good deeds to be destroyed and nullified. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Your loud voice was for the purpose of delivering a sermon (khutbah), and your case is separate from others.(9)

In the phrase «ان تحبط اعمالكم و انتم لاتشعرون» (lest your deeds become nullified while you perceive not), this point is hidden: that some actions have inherent consequences (āthār waḍ'ī), and those consequences are not dependent on whether we know about them or not. If a person drinks a poisonous liquid, he will be poisoned, even if he thinks it is water. If we touch a live wire, we will be electrocuted, even if we think it has no electricity. Some sins cause famine, earthquakes, shortening of life, humiliation, and disgrace, even if the person himself is unaware of the factor causing these consequences.

Although raising the voice before the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is disrespect to Him, which causes the nullification of deeds due to His high status, the Quran advises us to observe etiquette towards all people and speak softly. As Luqman عليه السلام tells his son: «واغضُض من صَوتك» (Lower your voice) and speak softly. Then He likened loud voices to the braying of donkeys: «انّ اَنكَر الاصوات لَصوتُ الحَمیر»(10) (Indeed, the most disagreeable of sounds is the voice of the donkeys). The issue of loud noise and noise pollution is one of the problems of human society today, which sometimes, in the name of weddings, sometimes in the name of mourning, sometimes in the mosque, and sometimes in the streets and sports fields, deprives people of peace.

Examples of Disrespect to the Prophet

A: On the verge of His passing, the Prophet of Islam requested a pen and paper and said: I want to say something and have you write it down so that you will never go astray after it! Among those present, the second Caliph said: The Prophet's illness has become severe and His words are worthless; you have the Quran and there is no need for writing. Commotion arose in agreement and disagreement. The Prophet said: Get up and leave.(11)

The late Allamah Sayyid Sharaf al-Din, author of Al-Murāja‘āt, states: Although the pen and paper were not brought and nothing was written, the unwritten line can still be read. Because by examining the narrations, we will see that the Prophet had stated this matter many times: that I am leaving you and bequeathing two precious things (Thaqalayn) that if you adhere to them, you will never go astray, and those two are the Quran and my Ahl al-Bayt. «انّى تارك فیكم الثقلین ما ان تمسّكتم بهما لن تضلّوا كتاب اللّه و عترتى اهل بیتى»(12). Therefore, what the Prophet intended here was adherence to the Quran and the Ahl al-Bayt.

B: When Imam Hassan Mujtaba عليه السلام was martyred and they wanted to bury Him beside the grave of His grandfather, the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, Aisha prevented it, and this caused the people's voices to rise. Imam Hussain عليه السلام commanded the people to silence by reciting the Ayah «لاتَرفَعوا اَصواتكم...» and said: Respect for the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله is necessary after His death just as it was during His lifetime.(13)

Sanctities in Islam

Throughout the world, despite differing beliefs, people show special respect to their great figures. They name cities, streets, universities, airports, schools, and institutions after them. In Islam, too, some individuals, and even some plants and inanimate objects, possess sanctity.

In Islam, the root of the sanctity and dignity (karāmah) of everything is its connection to the Holy Essence of Allah, and the greater this connection, the greater the sanctity, and we must preserve its special respect.

As for the sanctities:

  1. Allah is the source of holiness (Quds), and the polytheists, who considered others equal to God, will confess their deviation on the Day of Judgment and will say to their imaginary deities: The secret of our misery is that we considered you equal to the Lord of the worlds. «اذ نُسوّیكم بِربّ العالَمین»(14) The Quran speaks extensively about the glorification (tasbīḥ) and transcendence (tanzīh) of Allah, meaning we must accept for God that respect and sanctity such that no fault or imperfection can be imagined for Him. Not only His Essence, but His Name must also be purified. «سَبّح اسم ربّك الاعلى»(15)

  2. The Book of God also has special respect and sanctity. When Allah considers the Quran Aẓīm (Great),(16) we must exalt it; when He considers the Quran Karīm (Noble),(17) we must honor it; and since He considers the Quran Majīd (Glorious),(18) we must glorify it.

  3. The divine leaders, all the Prophets and their rightful successors, especially Muhammad صلى الله عليه وآله and His Ahl al-Bayt, possess a special status. In this Surah, some of the manners of interaction with the Prophet are stated: that we should not precede them, nor speak louder than them, and in other Ayahs, the command to send blessings (Ṣalawāt) upon the Prophet has been given.(19) Of course, we must pay attention to the point that even after the Prophet's passing, visiting Him (20) and visiting and respecting His successors and progeny, and all those who are related to Him in some way—especially the divine scholars (‘ulamā’ rabbānī) and just jurists (fuqahā’ ‘ādil) and Marāji‘ al-Taqlīd (Sources of Emulation), who, according to narrations, are the successors of the Prophet—is obligatory upon us. As we read in a Hadith: Whoever rejects the word of a just jurist is like one who has rejected the word of the Prophet's Ahl al-Bayt علیهم السلام, and whoever rejects their word is like one who has rejected the word of God.(21) Not only the person of the Prophets, but whatever relates to them possesses sanctity and dignity. We read in the Quran: The Ark (sandūq) in which Moses عليه السلام was placed as an infant and cast into the sea, and which later held the relics of Moses and the family of Moses, was so sacred that the angels carried it and it brought victory over enemies.(22)

  4. In Islam, parents enjoy special dignity and sanctity. In the Quran, five times, after the command for monotheism, kindness to parents is mentioned (23), and gratitude to them is placed alongside gratitude to Allah.(24) Respect for parents is such that a loving gaze towards them is an act of worship, and we are advised not to raise our voices louder than theirs. A journey that causes them distress is forbidden, and in such a journey, the prayer must be performed fully (not shortened).

  5. In Islam, certain times, such as the Night of Qadr (Laylat al-Qadr); certain places, such as the mosque; certain stones, such as the Black Stone (Hajar al-Aswad); certain waters, such as Zamzam; certain soils, such as the soil of Imam Hussain عليه السلام (Turbah); and certain garments, such as the Ihram clothing, are considered sacred, and special respect must be accorded to them. We read in the Quran: Prophet Moses, out of respect for the sacred valley, took off His shoes upon entering it. «فَاخلَع نَعلَیك انّك بِالوادِ المقدّس طُوى »(25) Al-Masjid al-Haram is a sacred place that a polytheist has no right to enter. «انّما المشركون نجس فلایَقربوا المسجد الحرام»(26) Places of worship and mosques are sacred, and when you go to the mosque, go beautifully and cleanly. «خُذوا زینَتكم عند كلّ مسجد»(27) And a person in a state of major ritual impurity (junub) or impurity has no right to stay in the mosque. «و لاجُنباً الاّ عابرى سبیل»(28) The mosque is so revered that individuals like Prophets Abraham, Ishmael, Zechariah, and Mary علیهم السلام were responsible for purifying it. «طَهّرا بَیتى»(29) Even the mother of Mary, who thought her child was a boy, vowed to make him a servant of Al-Masjid al-Aqsa. «انّى نَذَرتُ لَك ما فى بَطنى مُحرّراً»(30)

  6. The believing human also possesses sanctity and dignity, to the extent that the honor of a believer is greater than the Ka'bah, and harming or backbiting him is forbidden, defending his right is obligatory, and even after death, exhuming his grave is forbidden.

Messages

  1. To teach etiquette to others, we must also address them courteously. (In this Ayah, Allah addresses the believers with the phrase «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا» [O you who have believed].)

  2. The recommendation to respect elders is beautiful when it comes from others. (In this Ayah, the Prophet does not say: Do not speak louder than Me; it is Allah who commands: Do not speak louder than the Prophet.) «لا تَرفعوا اصواتكم»

  3. The status and position of individuals affect their deeds. (Disrespect shown by a believer towards the Prophet carries a heavy penalty.) «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا لا ترفعوا... ان تَحبط اعمالكم»

  4. Sometimes a person unknowingly strikes an axe at his own root. «تَحبط اعمالكم و انتم لاتشعرون»

Ayah

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَغُضُّونَ أَصْوَٰتَهُمْ عِندَ رَسُولِ ٱللَّهِ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱمْتَحَنَ ٱللَّهُ قُلُوبَهُمْ لِلتَّقْوَىٰ ۚ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ عَظِيمٌ (۳)
Indeed those who lower their voices in the presence of the Apostle of Allah—they are the ones whose hearts Allah has tested for Godwariness. For them will be forgiveness and a great reward. (3)

Key Points

Ghaḍḍ (غَضّ) means to lower or shorten. This word is used in the Quran both for lowering the voice and for lowering the gaze; as the Quran states regarding looking at non-mahrams (unrelated persons): «قل للمؤمنین یغضّوا من ابصارهم» (31)

If etiquette is necessary in speech, it is necessary a fortiori (even more so) in behavior and conduct. Speaking softly is a sign of a person's etiquette, humility, dignity, and calmness.

The Quran states in this Ayah: Allah tests the hearts of individuals to reveal the extent of their inner purity and piety. Because many people hypocritically express etiquette and humility, but are arrogant within.

Divine rewards are usually accompanied by the attributes Karīm (كَریم - Generous), Aẓīm (عَظیم - Great), Kabīr (كَبیر - Large), and Ghayr Mamnūn (غَیر مَمنُون - Unfailing/Continuous), and this is because the source of these rewards is the infinite mercy and grace of Allah.

Etiquette in Speech This Ayah describes the etiquette of speaking with the Messenger of Allah (Rasūl Allāh). Here, it is appropriate to mention the instructions that Islam provides regarding the type of speech, the speaker, and the manner of expression:

  1. Speech must be factual and truthful. «بِنَباءٍ یَقین» (32)
  2. Speech must be pleasing. «الطّیّب مِن القَول» (33)
  3. Speech must be eloquent and clear. «قَولاً بَلیغاً» (34)
  4. Some speech must be expressed gently. «قولاً لَیّناً» (35)
  5. Speech must be expressed nobly. «قَولاً كریماً» (36)
  6. Speech should be easy to accept and act upon. «قَولاً مَیسوراً» (37)
  7. Speech must contain no frivolity or falsehood. «اِجتَنِبوا قَول الزّور» (38), «عن اللَّغو مُعرضون» (39)
  8. Speech must be accompanied by action, otherwise it is blameworthy. «لِمَ تَقولون مالا تَفعلون» (40)

Messages

  1. We should both rebuke the wrongdoer and encourage the righteous. (In the previous Ayah, those who spoke loudly in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله were rebuked, and in this Ayah and subsequent Ayahs, the well-mannered individuals are encouraged.) «انّ الّذین یَغضّون... لهم مغفرة...»

  2. Temporary and momentary displays of etiquette do not indicate the depth of piety. «یَغضّون اصواتهم» (The imperfect verb form indicates continuity and permanence.)

  3. Wherever "forgiveness" (maghfirah) and "reward" (ajr) are mentioned in the Quran, forgiveness is mentioned first. This is because unless we are purified from sin, we cannot receive Divine favors. «لهم مغفرة و اجر عظیم»

  4. Faith and piety do not necessarily entail infallibility from sin. Many faithful and pious individuals may fall into error and sin, but they quickly compensate for it. «لهم مغفرة»

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يُنَادُونَكَ مِن وَرَآءِ ٱلْحُجُرَٰتِ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْقِلُونَ (٤)
Indeed those who call you from behind the apartments, most of them do not use their reason. (4)
وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ صَبَرُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ تَخْرُجَ إِلَيْهِمْ لَكَانَ خَيْرًا لَّهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٥)
Had they been patient until you came out for them, it would have been better for them, and Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (5)

Key Points

Islam places special importance on social etiquette (adab). In this Surah, a part of that etiquette is mentioned, and thus it has become famous as the Surah of Etiquette (Adab). Although the issue of calling the Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله from behind a wall or window is no longer relevant today, the lesson and principle derived from this incident are useful for us. We read in Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani that Ibn Abbas used to go to his teacher's house, but he would not knock on the door; he would wait until the teacher came out himself. They asked him: Why don't you knock? In response, he recited this Ayah: «و لو انّهم صَبَروا حتّى تَخرُجَ الیهم لَكان خَیراً لَهم» (If they had patience until you came out to them, it would have been better for them.) The rooms (hujurat) of the Prophet's house صلى الله علیه وآله were equal to the number of his wives, and each of them had an independent chamber (hujrah). These rooms were very simple and unadorned, and unfortunately, during the time of Walid ibn Abd al-Malik, they were destroyed and incorporated into the mosque. (41) I wish these chambers had remained intact so that the people of the world could see this simplicity and take a lesson! Imam Ali عليه السلام says: «لا عَقلَ لِمَن لا أدبَ له» (There is no intellect for one who has no etiquette), meaning, one who lacks etiquette is, in reality, not wise.

41) Tafsir Ruh al-Ma'ani, from the book Tabaqat Ibn Sa'd. 42) Ghurar al-Hikam.

Messages

1- Usually, lack of etiquette is a sign of lack of wisdom. «الّذین ینادونك... لا یعقلون»

2- Lack of etiquette towards heavenly leaders is subject to reproach and blame. «الّذین ینادونك... لایعقلون»

3- The home and family have a sanctuary (hurim), and others do not have the right to disturb them, even by calling them from outside the house. «ینادونك من وراء الحجرات اكثرهم لایعقلون»

4- The case of individuals who speak loudly out of habit (not based on insult or contempt) is separate from that of rude and malicious individuals. «اكثرهم لایعقلون»

5- We must respect the schedules and free time of others. The Prophet also needs peace and rest, and people should not disturb him at every moment. «لو انّهم صبروا...»

6- Social responsibilities should not prevent leaders from attending to their family affairs. «صبروا حتى تخرج الیهم»

7- The Noble Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله had set specific times for meeting people, and there was no need to shout from the street. «صبروا حتّى تخرج الیهم»

8- In dealing with rude and unwise individuals, we should approach them through the door of mercy, forgiveness, and kindness, and not cause them despair. While Allah reproaches those who called the Prophet by shouting, He mentions His forgiveness and mercy so that they do not become completely hopeless and have the opportunity to change their behavior. «لو انّهم صبروا... واللّه غفور رحیم»

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن جَآءَكُمْ فَاسِقٌۢ بِنَبَإٍ فَتَبَيَّنُوٓا۟ أَن تُصِيبُوا۟ قَوْمًۢا بِجَهَـٰلَةٍ فَتُصْبِحُوا۟ عَلَىٰ مَا فَعَلْتُمْ نَـٰدِمِينَ (٦)
O you who have faith! If a vicious character brings you some news, verify it, lest you should visit [harm] on some people out of ignorance, and then become regretful for what you have done. (6)

Key Points

Based on numerous narrations from both Shi'a and Sunni sources, this Ayah was revealed concerning Walid ibn Uqbah. This is because the Prophet sent him to collect Zakat from the tribe of Banu Mustaliq. However, since there was intense enmity between him and this tribe during the Age of Ignorance (Jahiliyyah), when the people of the tribe came out to meet him, he thought they had come with the intention of killing him. Therefore, he did not proceed further, returned to the Prophet, and reported that the people had refused to pay Zakat. The Prophet was distressed by this news and decided to punish them. The above Ayah was revealed and commanded investigation regarding the news brought by a fasiq (transgressor). It is clear that the transgression (fisq) of Walid ibn Uqbah was not known from the beginning, otherwise the Prophet would not have sent a transgressor as his representative to receive Zakat. Rather, after the lie Walid told about the tribe of Banu Mustaliq refusing to pay Zakat, this Ayah revealed his transgression so that the Prophet and the believers would not act based on his report. Imam Husayn عليه السلام, in a debate He had with Walid ibn Uqbah, said: "By God, I do not blame you for your enmity toward Ali ibn Abi Talib علیهما السلام, because Allah called Ali a believer and called you a transgressor (fasiq)." Then He recited the above Ayah. (43)

Question: In this Ayah, the command is given for investigation and verification, but in Ayah 12 of the same Surah, tajassus (spying) is considered forbidden. Can investigation and scrutiny be both forbidden and obligatory?

Answer: Where tajassus is forbidden, it concerns the private behavior of people that is unrelated to social life. But where it is obligatory, it is a case related to society, and we intend to take action based on it. If we do not investigate and verify for the sake of respecting an individual, the society might be exposed to sedition (fitnah) and chaos.

What is Fisq and who is a Fasiq?

Fisq linguistically means "to exit," and in Quranic terminology, it refers to exiting the straight path. This word is used in contrast to justice (adalah), and a fasiq is someone who commits a major sin and does not repent.

The word fisq, in various forms, has appeared fifty-four times in the Quran and has been used in different contexts and instances, including:

  1. Concerning the deviations of Pharaoh and his people: «انّهم كانوا قَوماً فاسقین» (44)
  2. Concerning two-faced individuals and hypocrites: «انّ المنافقین همُ الفاسقون» (45)
  3. Concerning those who harm the prophets and rebel against their commands: «قالوا یا موسى انّا لن ندخلها... القوم الفاسقین» (46)
  4. Concerning those who do not judge and rule according to Divine law: «و مَن لم یحكم بما اَنزلَ اللّه فَاولئك هم الفاسقون» (47)
  5. Concerning tricksters and deceivers: «...بما كانوا یَفسقون» (48)
  6. Concerning those who abandon the important duty of enjoining good and forbidding evil (Amr bil Ma'ruf wa Nahy anil Munkar): «اَنجَینا الّذین یَنهَونَ عن السوء و اَخَذنَا الّذین ظلموا... بما كانوا یَفسقون» (49)
  7. Concerning those who prefer home, trade, family, and material possessions over striving in the way of Allah: «ان كان آبائكم... اَحبّ... واللّه لا یَهدِى القومَ الفاسقین» (50)
  8. Concerning public sins, sexual deviations, and unlawful lusts. (The Quran gave the title fasiq to the people of Lot who committed sin openly and shamelessly): «رِجزاً من السماء بما كانوا یَفسقون» (51)
  9. Concerning the consumption of forbidden foods: «حُرّمت علیكم المَیتَةُ و الدّم... ذلكم فِسق» (52)
  10. Concerning slandering chaste women: «یَرمُونَ المحصنات... اولئك هم الفاسقون» (53)

Investigation, the Cure for Social Ills

Throughout history, the Prophets have dealt with people and societies afflicted by various social and moral diseases. Even today, despite all the significant advancements in human life, those diseases remain strong. Diseases such as:

  • Blind imitation of ancestors and following superstitious customs, traditions, and rituals.
  • Following baseless fantasies, rumors, prophecies, and dreams.
  • Judging and taking a stance without knowledge, praising or criticizing without knowledge, and writing or speaking without knowledge.

The healing remedy for all those diseases is investigation and verification, as mentioned in this Ayah. If society is characterized by precision, investigation, and verification, all these social scourges and calamities will be completely cured.

Similar to this Ayah is Ayah 94 of Surah An-Nisa, which states: «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا اذا ضربتم فى سبیل اللّه فتبیّنوا و لا تقولوا لمن ألقى الیكم السّلام لستَ مؤمناً تبتغون عرض الحیاة الدنیا فعند اللّه مغانم كثیرة كذلك كنتم من قبل فمَنّ اللّه علیكم فتبیّنوا اِنّ اللّه كان بما تعملون خبیراً»

O believers! Whenever you march forth in the way of Allah and travel, investigate and verify, and do not say to someone who offers you peace (or declares faith), "You are not a believer," and do not kill an innocent person for the sake of obtaining spoils. For with Allah are abundant gains. Thus were you before, but Allah conferred His favor upon you; so investigate. Indeed, Allah is ever Aware of what you do.

As recorded in history: After the Battle of Khaybar, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله sent a person named Usamah ibn Zayd along with a group of Muslims toward the Jews who were in one of the villages of Fadak, to invite them to Islam or to accept the conditions of Dhimmah (protected status). One of the Jews (named Mirdas), upon hearing the news, placed his property and family under the protection of a mountain and came out to meet the Muslims, saying, «لا اله الاّ اللّه محمّدٌ رَسولُ اللّه» (There is no god but Allah, Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah). Usamah, assuming that he had embraced Islam out of fear and that his conversion was not genuine, killed him. When the Messenger of Allah was informed, He became extremely distressed, and the above Ayah was revealed.

News in Islam

Islam has placed great emphasis on the manner of speaking and transmitting news, including:

A) The Quran severely criticizes those who hear any news and spread it without careful consideration, stating that they should refer the news to those capable of deduction, and only if its truth is established should they disseminate it. (54)

B) The Quran has specified a severe punishment for those who create anxiety in society by spreading false news. (55)

C) The Prophet of Islam صلى الله عليه وآله, in the last year of His blessed life during the Hajj pilgrimage, said: "Those who narrate false reports about me are many, and they will increase in the future. Whoever knowingly attributes a lie to me, his abode is the Fire. And whatever you hear from me, present it to the Quran and my Sunnah. If it conforms to them, accept it; otherwise, reject it." (56)

D) Imam Sadiq عليه السلام cursed a person who attributed false reports to his father, Imam Baqir عليه السلام, and said: "Whatever you hear from us, if you find a witness or evidence for it from the Quran or our other sayings, accept it; otherwise, do not accept it." (57)

E) Imam Ridha عليه السلام said: "The source of our sayings is the Quran and the Sunnah of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله." (58)

F) One of the most important Islamic sciences is Ilm al-Rijal (the Science of Narrators), which evaluates and investigates the individuals who transmit Hadith in order to distinguish correct reports from incorrect ones.

Messages

  1. Believing individuals must be investigative and discerning, not gullible and superficial. «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا... فتبیّنوا»
  2. Not all companions of the Prophet were just; there were also transgressors (fasiq) and hypocrites among them. «ان جاءكم فاسق»
  3. Exposing and disgracing individuals whose actions cause sedition is permissible. «ان جاءكم فاسق»
  4. The grounds for sedition are two things: the effort of the transgressor, and the gullibility of the believer. «الذین آمنوا ان جائكم فاسق... فتبیّنوا»
  5. The default principle in Islam is trust in people, but the case of someone whose transgression is clear to all is separate from ordinary individuals. «ان جاءكم فاسق... فتبیّنوا»
  6. Transgressors (fasiq) seek to promote false news and spread rumors. «جائكم فاسق بنبأ»
  7. Faith is incompatible with credulity. «آمنوا...فتبیّنوا»
  8. Do not delay investigation and verification. «فَتبیّنوا» (The letter fa indicates swift action.)
  9. Sometimes a transgressor speaks the truth; therefore, his word should not be rejected everywhere, but rather investigated. «فتبیّنوا»
  10. The Islamic society is exposed to informational attacks, and people must be vigilant. «ان جائكم... فتبیّنوا»
  11. In management, the remedy must be applied before the incident occurs. We must investigate first and then act. «فتبیّنوا ان تصیبوا قوماً بجهالة»
  12. Stating the philosophy and rationale behind Divine rulings increases people's motivation to carry out the commands. (The philosophy of investigation is avoiding the creation of sedition.) «فتبیّنوا ان تصیبوا»
  13. One of the goals of transgressing news agencies is to create sedition and disrupt the security of the system. «ان تصیبوا قوماً بجهالة»
  14. Acting based on an unverified report can lead a society to destruction. «ان تصیبوا قوماً»
  15. Hasty action without verification and investigation is a form of ignorance. «بجهالة»
  16. Acting upon Divine commands prevents regret. We must investigate lest we become regretful. «فتبیّنوا... نادمین»
  17. The end result of unmeasured action is regret. «نادمین»

Ayahs

وَٱعْلَمُوٓا۟ أَنَّ فِيكُمْ رَسُولَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ لَوْ يُطِيعُكُمْ فِى كَثِيرٍ مِّنَ ٱلْأَمْرِ لَعَنِتُّمْ وَلَـٰكِنَّ ٱللَّهَ حَبَّبَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنَ وَزَيَّنَهُۥ فِى قُلُوبِكُمْ وَكَرَّهَ إِلَيْكُمُ ٱلْكُفْرَ وَٱلْفُسُوقَ وَٱلْعِصْيَانَ ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلرَّٰشِدُونَ (۷)
Know that the Apostle of Allah is among you. Should he comply with you in many matters, you would surely suffer. But Allah has endeared faith to you and made it appealing in your hearts, and He has made hateful to you faithlessness, transgression and disobedience. It is such who are the right-minded— (7)
فَضْلًا مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَنِعْمَةً ۚ وَٱللَّهُ عَلِيمٌ حَكِيمٌ (۸)
a grace and blessing from Allah, and Allah is all-knowing, all-wise. (8)

Key Points

We read under the previous Ayah that Walid ibn 'Uqbah falsely reported to the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله that the people refused to pay Zakat. The people prepared to attack, and then the Ayah was revealed: If a transgressor brings you news, verify it. This Ayah states that, fundamentally, the people must follow the Prophet, and the divine leader should not be subject to immature excitements arising from the reports of transgressors.

In narrations, we read: If the people of faith are beloved to you, you are on the path of goodness, and if you are fond of sinners, there is no goodness in you.(59)

Based on narrations, one of the manifestations of faith in the phrase «حَبّبَ الیكم الایمان» (He has made faith beloved to you) is the love for Ali ibn Abi Talib علیهما السلام, and the meaning of disbelief (kufr), transgression (fusūq), and disobedience ('iṣyān) is enmity toward the Household of the Prophet علیهم السلام.(60)

In a hadith, we read: Those who do not consider the love for spiritual matters and the aversion to sin as a blessing have committed ingratitude (kufrān) for Allah's blessings; their deeds are few, and their efforts are wasted.(61)

Attaining growth (rushd) and perfection is a divine gift to the prophets: «آتَینا ابراهِیم رُشدَه» (We gave Abraham his guidance/growth) (62). And their duty is to guide others to growth: «یا قوم اتّبِعون اَهدِكم سَبیلَ الرّشاد» (O my people, follow me, I will guide you to the path of rectitude/growth) (63). They themselves also sought growth, just as Prophet Moses went after Khidr in the deserts to attain growth through his knowledge: «هل اتّبعك على أن تُعلّمَن ممّا عُلّمتَ رُشداً» (May I follow you on condition that you teach me some of the guidance/growth you have been taught?) (64). Faith is the basis for growth: «ولیؤمنوا بى لعلّهم یَرشُدون» (So let them believe in Me, that they may be rightly guided/grow) (65). And heavenly books are the means of growth: «یَهدى اِلىَ الرُّشد» (It guides to rectitude/growth) (66). In this Ayah, too, true believers are introduced as people of growth: «اولئك هم الراشدون» (These are the rightly guided/those who have attained growth). In any case, the meaning of growth (rushd) in the Quran is spiritual growth, and it is short-sightedness for a person to consider growth only in worldly successes.

In the management and policy-making of society, if the decision-maker is not infallible (ma'sūm) or just, it leads to a tyranny in which there is no growth: «و ما اَمرُ فِرعونَ بِرَشید» (And the command of Pharaoh was not rightly guided/did not lead to growth) (67). But if the decision-maker is infallible or just, the ground for growth is prepared. «اولئك هم الرّاشِدون»

Messages

  1. If we wish not to regret, we must refer to the teachings of the prophets. (In the previous Ayah, there was talk of remorse and regret, and this Ayah states: Refer to the Prophet so that you are safeguarded from regrettable actions.) «نادِمین - و اعلموا انّ فیكم رسول اللّه»
  2. The presence of the Prophet and divine leaders is a special privilege for society. «فیكم رسول اللَّه»
  3. The leader must be accessible to the people. «انّ فیكم رسول اللَّه»
  4. Expecting the Prophet to consult with the people is acceptable, but expecting the Prophet to obey the people is an undue expectation. «لو یطیعكم... لَعَنتّم...»
  5. The problems of societies arise from distancing themselves from the school of the prophets and acting upon personal tastes and desires. «لو یطیعكم... لَعَنتّم...»
  6. The leader of the Islamic society must have independence of judgment. «لو یطیعكم... لَعَنتّم...» (The enemy also desires these types of problems, which arise from the weakness of the leader's judgment, as we read elsewhere: «ودّوا ما عَنِتّم...» (They wish you would suffer hardship...) (68)).
  7. In cases where there is no ruling from Allah and His Messenger, consulting with the people and following them is acceptable. «فى كثیرٍ من الامر»
  8. Inclination toward religion is an innate matter that Allah has deposited within humanity. «حَبّبَ الیكم الایمان...»
  9. Faith is a matter of the heart that is incompatible with coercion and compulsion. «حَبّبَ الیكم الایمان»
  10. Faith is the adornment of hearts. «و زَیّنَه فى قلوبكم» (and has made it beautiful in your hearts), just as mountains, seas, minerals, flowers, waterfalls, and everything on the earth are the adornment of the earth. «انّا جَعلنا ما على الارض زینةً لها» (Indeed, We have made what is on the earth an adornment for it) (69). (Yes, the adornment of man is spiritual perfection, while material manifestations are the adornment of the earth.)
  11. Tawallī (loving the good) and Tabarrī (disassociating from the evil) must be side by side; if we love the people of faith, we must also be averse to the people of disbelief (kufr), transgression (fisq), and disobedience ('iṣyān). «حَبّبَ الیكم الایمان... كَرّهَ الیكم الكفر...»
  12. Inner denial and corrupt motivation are the prelude to rebellion. First, He mentioned: «الكفر» (disbelief), and then «الفسوق و العصیان» (transgression and disobedience).
  13. The afflictions of faith are disbelief (kufr), transgression (fusūq), and disobedience ('iṣyān). «حَبّب الیكم الایمان... كرّه الیكم الكفر و الفسوق و العصیان»
  14. Aversion to evil is an innate matter, «كَرّهَ الیكم الكفر...» (He has made disbelief hateful to you...), and this aversion is the source of human growth. «اولئك هم الرّاشدون»
  15. Those who do not have aversion to disbelief, transgression, and disobedience do not attain growth. «كَرّهَ... اولئك هم الرّاشِدون»
  16. Among the great divine blessings are the love for faith and the aversion to disbelief, transgression, and disobedience. «حَبّبَ الیكم الایمان... كَرّهَ الیكم الكفر... فَضلاً من اللَّه و نِعمة» (as a favor from Allah and a blessing).
  17. Allah's favor is based on His knowledge and wisdom, not without criteria or extravagance. «فضلاً... و اللّه علیم حكیم»

Ayah

وَإِن طَآئِفَتَانِ مِنَ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ٱقْتَتَلُوا۟ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا ۖ فَإِنۢ بَغَتْ إِحْدَىٰهُمَا عَلَى ٱلْأُخْرَىٰ فَقَـٰتِلُوا۟ ٱلَّتِى تَبْغِى حَتَّىٰ تَفِىٓءَ إِلَىٰٓ أَمْرِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ فَإِن فَآءَتْ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَهُمَا بِٱلْعَدْلِ وَأَقْسِطُوٓا۟ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُقْسِطِينَ (۹)
If two groups of the faithful fight one another, make peace between them. But if one party of them aggresses against the other, fight the one which aggresses until it returns to Allah’s ordinance. Then, if it returns, make peace between them fairly, and do justice. Indeed Allah loves the just. (9)

Key Points

Although the requirement of faith is avoidance of conflict and confrontation among believers, believers are not infallible (معصوم) such that they never commit errors. Sometimes, saying a sentence or performing an action may lead to conflict among them. Therefore, one must be prepared so that if such confrontations occur, while extinguishing the fire of discord (فتنه), the right of the oppressed (مظلوم) is not trampled upon, and the oppressor (ظالم) is dealt with in such a way that the transgression is never repeated. We read in a Hadith: Help your religious brother, whether he is an oppressor or oppressed. If he is oppressed, help him obtain his right; and if he is an oppressor, help him by preventing his oppression. (70)

This Ayah is the Ayah for the recitation of which two hands were severed. During the Battle of Jamal (the Camel), although Hadrat Ali عليه السلام forbade the opponents from starting the war, the supporters of Aisha did not listen, and the Imam complained to Allah that the people were disobeying. He then took the Quran in his hand and asked: Who will recite the Ayah, "و ان طائفتان من المؤمنین..." (And if two parties of the believers...) for the people? A person named Muslim Mujashi'i said: I will recite this Ayah loudly. The Imam said: They will cut off your hands and martyr you. He replied: "هذا قلیلٌ فى ذاتِ اللَّه" (This is little for the sake of Allah); my martyrdom in the way of God is nothing. He then took the Quran and stood before Aisha's army, inviting the people to faith in Allah, hoping that no blood would be shed and no war would take place. However, they attacked him and cut off his right hand. He took the Quran in his left hand; they also severed his left hand. He held the Quran with his teeth. Finally, they martyred this reciter of the Quran. Hadrat Ali عليه السلام ordered the attack after his martyrdom. (71) Yes, first, the conclusive argument (اتمام حجّت) must be established by referencing the Quran, and then one must bravely struggle for the goal until the last breath.

Justice (عدالت) In this Ayah, the establishment of peace and reconciliation among people based on justice is mentioned three times: "فاصلِحوا بینهما بالعَدل و اقسِطوا انّ اللَّه یحبّ المقسطین" (So make peace between them with justice and act justly. Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly.)

Fundamentally, creation is based on truth and justice. "بالعَدلِ قامَت السّموات" (The heavens stand by justice) (72), and the mission of the prophets was so that people might uphold justice. "لِیَقومَ النّاس بِالقِسط" (So that mankind may uphold justice) (73).

In today's societies, the realization of justice is synonymous with adherence to law, and injustice is synonymous with evasion of law. But which law? A law made by human hands just like us? A law that is constantly changing and evolving? A law in which the interests of individuals and parties are hidden, arising from immature thoughts and limited information, and enacted under the influence of threats, temptations, and the desires of themselves and others? A law whose legislator engages in various forms of law evasion and does not act upon it himself? Can these laws possess sanctity and dignity for humanity and bring justice and security to society? What can establish peace and justice in society and be free from all error and defect are the Divine laws presented to humanity by the Prophets. Laws that the Creator of man has enacted based on infinite knowledge, wisdom, and grace, and whose bearer is the first one to act upon them.

70) Wasā'il, Vol. 12, p. 212. 71) Bihār, Vol. 32, p. 175. 72) Bihār, Vol. 33, p. 493. 73) Al-Hadid, 25.

Messages

1- The occurrence of conflict and confrontation among believers is a spark, not a continuous flow; it is temporary, not permanent. "اِقتَتلوا" (they fought) indicates the emergence of conflict, not its continuation; otherwise, it would have said: "یَقتَتلون" (they are fighting).

2- Muslims are responsible toward one another, and indifference toward conflicts is unacceptable. "فَاصلِحوا بینهما" (So make peace between them).

3- To reconcile and establish peace among Muslims, we must act quickly and without delay. (The letter fa (ف) in "فَاصلِحوا" indicates speed.)

4- If one of the two conflicting groups commits aggression, the Islamic Ummah must mobilize against it. "فَاِن بَغَتْ... فقاتلوا الّتى تَبغى" (But if one of them transgresses... then fight the one that transgresses).

5- To establish security and justice, even if necessary, the rebellious Muslims must be killed. Yes, the blood of the aggressor has no value. "فَاِن بَغتْ... فقاتلوا..." (But if one of them transgresses... then fight...).

6- Violence must be met with violence. "فَاِن بَغتْ... فقاتلوا" (But if one of them transgresses... then fight).

7- In suppressing the aggressor, we must not evade or be lenient. "فقاتلوا" (The letter fa (فاء) is a sign of acceleration/speed).

8- War and fighting (قتال) in Islam have a sacred goal. "حتّى تَفى ء الى امر اللّه" (until it complies with the command of Allah).

9- The duration of the struggle is until the goal is reached, "حتّى تَفى ء الى امر اللَّه" (until it complies with the command of Allah), and it has no specific hour, month, or date. It is like the duration of a patient's visit to the doctor and consumption of medicine, which must continue until health is restored.

10- In Islamic struggles, personal, tribal, partisan goals, or revenge, showing off, or intimidating others are not the issue; rather, the goal is the return of the aggressor to the path of Allah. "حتّى تَفى ء الى امر اللَّه" (until it complies with the command of Allah).

11- In the stage of conflict where the aggressor is unknown, the effort must be directed toward extinguishing the root of the discord (فتنه) and establishing peace, "فَاصلحوا بینهما" (So make peace between them). But in the subsequent stage, when the aggressor and rebel are identified, peace must be directed toward defending the oppressed and securing his right from the oppressor. "فَاصلحوا بینهما بالعَدل" (So make peace between them with justice).

12- The duty of Muslims differs in various situations; sometimes it is reconciliation, and sometimes it is war. (In this Ayah, the word "اصلحوا" (make peace) is mentioned twice, and the word "قاتلوا" (fight) is also mentioned.)

13- Wherever there is a storm of instincts, anger, and a dangerous pitfall, successive recommendations are necessary. "بالعَدل, اقسطوا... یحبّ المقسِطین" (with justice, act justly... He loves those who act justly).

14- Peace is valuable only if the right reaches its owner; otherwise, a humiliating, deadly, and imposed compromise is worthless. "اصلحوا... و اقسطوا" (Make peace... and act justly).

15- Wherever enduring difficulties is necessary, use the leverage of love. "انّ اللَّه یُحبّ المقسطین" (Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly).

16- Reconciling two warring parties without establishing justice is not beloved by Allah. "انّ اللّه یحبّ المقسِطین" (Indeed, Allah loves those who act justly).

Ayah

إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا۟ بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (۱٠)
The faithful are indeed brothers. Therefore, make peace between your brothers and be wary of Allah, so that you may receive [His] mercy. (10)

Key Points

This Ayah considers the relationship between believers to be like the relationship between two brothers, and this expression contains several points, including: A) The friendship between two brothers is deep and lasting. B) The friendship between two brothers is mutual, not one-sided. C) The friendship between two brothers is based on innate nature (fitra) and disposition, not material or worldly attractions. D) Two brothers are united against a stranger and are each other's strength. E) The origin and root of the two brothers are one. F) Attention to brotherhood leads to forgiveness and overlooking faults. G) One is happy in his joy and sad in his sorrow.

Today, words like companion (rafiq), friend (dust), fellow citizen, and compatriot are used to express affection, but Islam uses the word brother, which is the deepest of terms. In a hadith, two religious brothers are likened to two hands that wash each other.(74)

In this Ayah and the previous Ayah, the command "أَصْلِحُوا" (make peace/reconcile) has been repeated three times, which indicates Islam's attention to establishing peace and friendship among members of society. In the previous Ayah, He said: "فَأَصْلِحُوا... وَأَقْسِطُوا" (So make peace... and act justly), meaning establish peace with justice. And this Ayah says: "فَأَصْلِحُوا... وَاتَّقُوا" (So make peace... and fear Allah), meaning fear Allah when establishing peace. If they accept you as a mediator, consider Allah and judge, lest your reconciliation lead to oppression and injustice against one of the parties.

Brotherhood (Akhuwwah) One of the distinctions of Islam is that it begins reforms from the root. For example, He says: "إِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا" (75) (Indeed, all honor belongs to Allah), so why do you seek honor from this person and that? Or He says: "إِنَّ الْقُوَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعًا" (76) (Indeed, all power belongs to Allah), so why do you revolve around one person every hour!? In this Ayah too, He says: All believers are brothers to one another. Then He says: Now that you are all brothers, why the estrangement and conflict? Be friends with one another. Therefore, to reform the behavior of the individual and society, their intellectual and ideological foundation must be reformed.

The concept of brotherhood (Akhuwwah) and the use of this term is one of the innovations of Islam. In the early days of Islam, the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله was present with seven hundred and forty people in the region of "Nakhilah" when Gabriel descended and said: Allah has established a pact of brotherhood among the angels. The Prophet also established a pact of brotherhood among his companions, and everyone became brothers with someone else. For example: Abu Bakr with Umar, Uthman with Abdur-Rahman, Salman with Abu Dharr, Talhah with Zubayr, Mus'ab with Abu Ayyub al-Ansari, Hamzah with Zayd ibn Harithah, Abu Darda' with Bilal, Ja'far al-Tayyar with Mu'adh ibn Jabal, Miqdad with Ammar, Aisha with Hafsah, Umm Salamah with Safiyyah, and the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله Himself established a pact of brotherhood with Ali عليه السلام.(77)

During the Battle of Uhud, the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله ordered two martyrs, Abdullah ibn Amr and Amr ibn al-Jamuh, between whom brotherhood had been established, to be buried in one grave.(78)

Kinship brotherhood will one day be severed, "فَلَا أَنْسَابَ بَيْنَهُمْ" (79) (there will be no kinship ties between them), but religious brotherhood is lasting, even on the Day of Judgment. "إِخْوَانًا عَلَى سُرُرٍ مُتَقَابِلِينَ" (80) (as brothers, facing each other on couches).

Friendship and brotherhood must be solely for the sake of Allah. If someone befriends another for the sake of the world, he will not attain what he expects, and they will become enemies to each other on the Day of Judgment.(81) The Quran says: On that Day, friends will become enemies to one another, except the righteous (Muttaqin). "الْأَخِلَّاءُ يَوْمَئِذٍ بَعْضُهُمْ لِبَعْضٍ عَدُوٌّ إِلَّا الْمُتَّقِينَ" (82)

What is more important than acquiring a brother is maintaining brotherhood. Narrations severely criticize those who abandon their religious brothers, and it is recommended that even if your brothers distance themselves from you, you should maintain contact with them. "صِلْ مَن قَطَعَكَ" (Maintain ties with him who severs them from you).(83)

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: A believer is the brother of a believer, like a single body, such that if one part of it becomes ill, the entire body is distressed.(84)

Rights of Brotherhood The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: A Muslim has thirty rights over his Muslim brother, which each must fulfill, including: 1. Forgiving him and showing kindness to him, 2. Concealing his secrets, 3. Compensating for his mistakes, 4. Accepting his apology, 5. Defending him against those who wish him ill, 6. Wishing good for him (sincerity/counsel), 7. Fulfilling the promises made to him, 8. Visiting him when he is ill, 9. Attending his funeral, 10. Accepting his invitation and gift, 11. Rewarding his gifts, 12. Thanking him for his services, 13. Striving to help him, 14. Protecting his honor (and family), 15. Fulfilling his need, 16. Mediating to solve his problems, 17. Guiding him to his lost item, 18. Congratulating him upon his sneeze, 19. Responding to his greeting (Salam), 20. Respecting his speech and words, 21. Preparing his gift well, 22. Accepting his oath, 23. Loving his friend and not showing enmity toward him, 24. Not abandoning him during calamities, 25. Desiring for him whatever he desires for himself, and so on.(85)

The Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, after reciting the Ayah "إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ" (The believers are but brothers), said: The blood of Muslims is equal, and if one of them grants refuge or security to someone, others must adhere to that commitment, and they must all mobilize against the common enemy. "وَهُمْ يَدٌ عَلَى مَنْ سِوَاهُمْ" (And they are one hand against those besides them).(86)

Peace and Reconciliation in the Quran In the Quran, the terms Sulh (peace), "وَالصُّلْحُ خَيْرٌ" (87) (and reconciliation is better), Islah (reconciliation/reform), "وَأَصْلِحُوا ذَاتَ بَيْنِكُمْ" (88) (and set right matters between you), Ta'lif al-Qulub (uniting hearts), "فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ" (89) (and He brought your hearts together), and Silm (peace/submission), "ادْخُلُوا فِي السِّلْمِ كَافَّةً" (90) (Enter into peace, all of you), demonstrate Islam's attention to peace, harmony, and a sweet, peaceful life.

One of the divine blessings that Allah mentions in the Quran is the affection (ulfa) among the hearts of Muslims. As Allah addresses the Muslims, He says: "كُنْتُمْ أَعْدَاءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ" (91) (You were enemies, and He brought your hearts together). Recall that before Islam, you were enemies to one another, and then Allah established affection among your hearts. For instance, there was conflict and strife between the tribes of Aws and Khazraj for one hundred and twenty years, and Islam established peace between them.

Reconciliation and making peace are considered a means of receiving forgiveness and mercy from Allah. "إِنْ تُصْلِحُوا وَتَتَّقُوا فَإِنَّ اللَّهَ كَانَ غَفُورًا رَحِيمًا" (92) (If you reconcile and fear Allah, then indeed, Allah is ever Forgiving and Merciful). And whoever performs a good mediation among Muslims will receive a worthy reward. "مَنْ يَشْفَعْ شَفَاعَةً حَسَنَةً يَكُنْ لَهُ نَصِيبٌ مِنْهَا" (93) (Whoever intercedes with a good intercession will have a share of it).

Islam has established special rulings for reconciliation among people, including: 1. Lying, which is a major sin, has no fault or sin if it is spoken for the sake of peace and reconciliation. "لَا كِذْبَ عَلَى الْمُصْلِحِ" (There is no lie upon the reconciler).(94) 2. Whispering and speaking secretly, which is an act of Satan and is forbidden,(95) is not prohibited if it is for the purpose of reconciliation and making peace. "لَا خَيْرَ فِي كَثِيرٍ مِنْ نَجْوَاهُمْ إِلَّا مَنْ أَمَرَ بِصَدَقَةٍ أَوْ مَعْرُوفٍ أَوْ إِصْلَاحٍ بَيْنَ النَّاسِ" (96) (There is no good in much of their private conversation, except for whoever enjoins charity or what is right or reconciliation between people). 3. Although fulfilling an oath is obligatory and breaking it is forbidden, if someone swears an oath not to engage in reconciliation and making peace between two people, Islam permits the breaking of this oath. "وَلَا تَجْعَلُوا اللَّهَ عُرْضَةً لِأَيْمَانِكُمْ أَنْ... تُصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ النَّاسِ" (97) (And do not make [your oath by] Allah an obstacle against your oaths to... make peace between people). 4. Although acting upon a will (wasiyyah) is obligatory and abandoning it is forbidden, if acting upon the will causes strife and resentment among people, Islam permits the will to be abandoned so that peace and harmony may prevail among the people. "فَمَنْ خَافَ مِنْ مُوصٍ جَنَفًا أَوْ إِثْمًا فَأَصْلَحَ بَيْنَهُمْ فَلَا إِثْمَ عَلَيْهِ" (98) (But if one fears from the testator some error or sin and reconciles between them, there is no sin upon him).

Messages

1- The relationship of brotherhood depends on faith. (Economic, political, racial, geographical, historical, and other issues cannot create the spirit of brotherhood among people.) "إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ"

The souls of wolves and dogs are separate United are the souls of Allah's lions.

2- Brotherhood based on faith is not conditioned by age, occupation, literacy, or income. "إِنَّمَا الْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ"

3- No one should consider himself superior to others. "إِخْوَةٌ" (Yes, there is superiority between parents and children, but among brothers, there is equality.)

4- To establish peace and reconciliation, we should use affectionate and motivating words. "إِخْوَةٌ فَأَصْلِحُوا"

5- Preventing conflict and taking action to reconcile and establish peace is the duty of everyone, not just a specific group. "فَأَصْلِحُوا"

6- The reconciler should also consider himself the brother of the conflicting parties. "فَأَصْلِحُوا بَيْنَ أَخَوَيْكُمْ"

7- Reconciliation and making peace have pitfalls that must be guarded against. "فَأَصْلِحُوا... وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ"

8- Peace and harmony are the prelude to the descent of Divine Mercy. "فَأَصْلِحُوا... تُرْحَمُونَ"

9- A society without piety is deprived of receiving Divine Mercy. "وَاتَّقُوا اللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ"

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا يَسْخَرْ قَوْمٌ مِّن قَوْمٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونُوا۟ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُمْ وَلَا نِسَآءٌ مِّن نِّسَآءٍ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُنَّ خَيْرًا مِّنْهُنَّ ۖ وَلَا تَلْمِزُوٓا۟ أَنفُسَكُمْ وَلَا تَنَابَزُوا۟ بِٱلْأَلْقَـٰبِ ۖ بِئْسَ ٱلِٱسْمُ ٱلْفُسُوقُ بَعْدَ ٱلْإِيمَـٰنِ ۚ وَمَن لَّمْ يَتُبْ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ (۱۱)
O you who have faith! Let not any people ridicule another people: it may be that they are better than they are; nor let women [ridicule] women: it may be that they are better than they are. And do not defame one another, nor insult one another by [calling] nicknames. How evil are profane names subsequent to faith! As for those who are not penitent [of their past conduct]—such are the wrongdoers. (11)

Key Points

Lamz is fault-finding face-to-face, and Hamz is fault-finding behind one's back (99). Tanabuz is naming or calling others by bad titles (100). In the previous Ayah, the issue of brotherhood among believers was discussed, and in this Ayah, matters that destroy brotherhood are presented. Similarly, in the previous Ayahs, the discussion was about peace and reconciliation; in this Ayah, it refers to some factors and roots of discord and conflict, namely mockery, humiliation, and calling by bad names. Indeed, one of the blessings of acting upon the commands of Islam is purifying the environment and preventing social conflicts. Perhaps it can be said that the mention of mockery and humiliation in this Ayah is by way of example, and any speech or action that damages Islamic fraternity and brotherhood is forbidden. Although women were included in the word qawm (people/group), their separate mention indicates the greater danger of mockery and humiliation among them.

Mocking and Ridiculing Others

Mocking and ridiculing others is outwardly one sin, but inwardly it is several sins; sins such as humiliation, degradation, exposing faults, sowing discord, backbiting, malice, sedition, provocation, revenge, and taunting others are hidden within it.

Roots of Mockery

  1. Sometimes mockery arises from wealth, about which the Quran says: «وَیلٌ لِكلّ هُمَزة لُمَزة، الّذى جَمع مالا و عَدّده» (101) Woe to him who, because of the wealth he has accumulated, finds fault with others, whether face-to-face or behind their back.
  2. Sometimes the root of ridicule is knowledge and academic degrees, about which the Quran says concerning this group: «فَرِحوا بما عِندهم مِن العِلم و حاقَ بهم ما كانوا به یَستهزؤن» (102) They rejoiced in the knowledge they possessed, and they were encompassed by that which they used to ridicule.
  3. Sometimes the root of mockery is physical ability. The disbelievers used to say: «مَن اَشدُّ مِنّا قُوّة» (103) Who is greater than us in strength?
  4. Sometimes the motive for mocking others is impressive social titles and statuses. The disbelievers used to humiliate the poor who accompanied the prophets and said: «ما نَراك اتّبعك الاّ الّذین هم أراذِلُنا» (104) We do not see anyone following you except those who are the lowest among us.
  5. Sometimes others are mocked under the pretext of fun and entertainment.
  6. Sometimes greed for wealth and position causes criticism of others accompanied by mockery. A group used to find fault with the Prophet صلى الله علیه وآله regarding Zakat (charity). The Quran says: «و منهم مَن یَلمزُك فى الصّدقات فان اُعطوا منها رَضوا و اِن لم یُعطوا منها اذا هم یَسخَطون» (105) The root of this criticism is greed; if they are given some of that Zakat, they are pleased, but if they are not given any, they become angry and continue to find fault.
  7. Sometimes the root of mockery is ignorance and foolishness. When Prophet Moses (Musa) عليه السلام commanded the killing of a cow to resolve a dispute and conflict, the Children of Israel said: Are you mocking us? Moses عليه السلام said: «أعوذُ باللّه اَن اَكونَ من الجاهلین» (106) I seek refuge in Allah from being among the ignorant. That is, mockery arises from ignorance and foolishness, and I am not ignorant.
  8. Sometimes the source of mockery is hypocrisy, fault-finding, and character assassination. «یلمزون المطّوّعین من المؤمنین فى الصّدقات و الّذین لایجدون الا جهدهم فیسخرون منهم» (107)

One of the blessed actions of the Messenger of Allah صلى الله علیه وآله after his appointment was changing the names of individuals and places that had ugly names (108). Because a bad name is a means for mockery and humiliation.

Aqil, the brother of Imam Ali عليه السلام, entered upon Mu'awiyah. Mu'awiyah, intending to humiliate him, said: Greetings to him whose uncle is Abu Lahab, cursed by Allah, and recited: «تبّت یدا ابى لهب» Aqil immediately replied: Greetings to him whose aunt is such, and recited: «و امراته حمّالة الحطب» (Abu Lahab's wife was the daughter of Mu'awiyah's paternal aunt.) (109)

Messages

1- Faith is incompatible with mocking the servants of Allah. «یا ایّها الّذین آمنوا لایسخر»

2- He who prevents people from insulting others should not use an insulting manner of speech Himself. «لایسخر قوم من قوم» (He did not say: «لاتسخروا» meaning, 'O you who are mockers.')

3- Mockery is the key to sedition, malice, and enmity. «لایسخر قوم...» (The prohibition of mockery comes after the discussion of brotherhood, peace, and reconciliation in the previous Ayahs.)

4- In the method of propagation, where an issue is important or the audience is diverse, the points must be repeated. «قوم من قوم... ولانساء من نساء»

5- The root of mockery is the feeling of self-superiority, which the Quran eradicates by stating: You should not consider yourselves better than others; perhaps they are better than you. «عسى ان یكونوا خیرا»

6- We are not aware of people's inner reality or their ultimate fate, so we should not be superficial, shallow-minded, or focused only on the present. «عسى ان یكونوا خیراً...»

7- Finding fault with others is, in reality, finding fault with oneself. «و لاتلمزوا انفسكم»

8- Reporting the faults of others leads to the discovery of one's own faults. «و لاتلمزوا انفسكم»

9- Calling someone by a bad name does not remain one-sided; sooner or later, the issue becomes mutual. «لاتنابزوا بالالقاب» (Tanabazu denotes a reciprocal action.)

10- Mockery and calling by bad names are sins and require repentance (Tawbah). «و من لم یتب فاولئك هم الظالمون» (Of course, repentance is not merely verbal; the repentance for humiliation is honoring the person.)

11- Mockery is a transgression against the sanctity of individuals, and if the mocker does not repent, he is an oppressor (Zalim). «فاولئك هم الظالمون»

Ayah

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱجْتَنِبُوا۟ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِنَّ بَعْضَ ٱلظَّنِّ إِثْمٌ ۖ وَلَا تَجَسَّسُوا۟ وَلَا يَغْتَب بَّعْضُكُم بَعْضًا ۚ أَيُحِبُّ أَحَدُكُمْ أَن يَأْكُلَ لَحْمَ أَخِيهِ مَيْتًا فَكَرِهْتُمُوهُ ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ تَوَّابٌ رَّحِيمٌ (۱۲)
O you who have faith! Avoid much suspicion; indeed some suspicions are sins. And do not spy on one another or backbite. Will any of you love to eat the flesh of his dead brother? You would hate it. Be wary of Allah; indeed Allah is all-clement, all-merciful. (12)

Key Points

Following the previous Ayah, this Ayah also refers to factors such as suspicion, spying, and backbiting, which disrupt peace, harmony, and brotherhood among believers.

In the Quran, good suspicion is recommended, and bad suspicion towards others is prohibited. In Surah An-Noor, we read: «لولا اِذ سَمِعتُموه ظَنّ المؤمنونَ و المؤمنات باَنفسِهم خَیراً»(110) Why, when you heard it, did not the believing men and believing women think good of one another?

Just as the ninth Ayah of this Surah held all Muslims responsible for fighting the rebellious and aggressive party to ensure the security and preservation of people's lives, this Ayah prohibits suspicion, spying, and backbiting to preserve people's honor.

Today, legal experts worldwide speak of human rights, but Islam pays attention to issues such as mockery, humiliation, spying, and backbiting, which legal experts overlook. No sin has been likened to ruthless predation like backbiting. Indeed, even predatory animals do not attack their own kind.

Types of Suspicion and Bad Conjecture Suspicion has types, some of which are praiseworthy and some blameworthy: 1. Suspicion of Allah, as we read in a Hadith: A person who does not marry for fear of living expenses, in reality, has suspicion of Allah; it is as if he imagines that if He is alone, Allah is capable of providing His sustenance, but if he has a spouse, Allah is not capable. This suspicion is prohibited. 2. Suspicion of people, which is prohibited in this Ayah. 3. Suspicion of self, which is praiseworthy. For a person should not have good suspicion of himself and consider all his actions flawless. Imam Ali عليه السلام, in enumerating the characteristics of the pious in Khutbah Hammam, states: One of the perfections of pious individuals is that they have suspicion of themselves. Indeed, individuals who consider themselves flawless actually have little light of knowledge and faith, and with little light, a person sees nothing. If you enter a large hall with a flashlight, you will only see large objects, but if you enter the hall with a strong light, like a searchlight, you can see even a matchstick or a pin if it is there. Individuals whose light of faith is weak only see major sins and thus sometimes say: We haven't killed anyone! We haven't climbed anyone's wall! And they consider sin only these types of actions. But if the light of faith is strong, they see all their minor slips and lament and seek forgiveness before Allah. If someone is optimistic about himself, he will never progress. He is like someone who constantly looks behind him at the paths traveled and becomes proud of them, but if he looks ahead and sees the paths untraveled, he will know that the untraveled paths are several times greater than the traveled ones!

It is necessary to note that the meaning of good suspicion is not simplicity, gullibility, superficiality, or negligence regarding plots and mischief. The Islamic Ummah must never become negligent due to misplaced good suspicion and fall into the trap of hunters.

Backbiting and its Consequences Backbiting is when a person says something in the absence of another person that people were unaware of, and which would upset that person if he heard it.(111)

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said: Leave the dead alone and do not recount their bad deeds; when someone dies, mention their good deeds.(112)

Imam Sadiq عليه السلام said: The backbiter, if he repents, is the last person to enter Paradise, and if he does not repent, he is the first person taken to Hell.(113)

Imam Reza عليه السلام quotes Imam Sajjad عليه السلام that whoever protects himself from violating the honor of Muslims, Allah will overlook his slips on the Day of Judgment.(114)

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Whoever backbites a man or woman, his prayer and fasting will not be accepted for forty days.(115)

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: On the Day of Judgment, when a person's record of deeds is handed to him, some will say: Why are our good deeds not recorded in it? They will be told: Allah neither diminishes anything nor forgets anything, but your good deeds have been destroyed because of the backbiting you committed. Conversely, other individuals will see many good deeds in their record and assume the file is not theirs. They will be told: Due to being backbitten, the good deeds of the person who backbit you have been recorded for you.(116)

Question: Why do narrations state that the punishment for a moment of backbiting is the destruction of a person's years of worship? Is this type of punishment just? Answer: Just as the backbiter destroys in a moment the honor that the believer acquired over years, Allah also destroys the worship that he acquired over years. Therefore, the erasure of years of worship is a just punishment.

The Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله said during his last journey to Mecca: The blood, property, and honor of a Muslim are sacred, just like this month of Dhul-Hijjah and these days of Hajj are sacred.(117)

In narrations, the name of the backbiter is mentioned alongside someone who habitually drinks wine. «تحرم الجَنّة على المغتاب و مُدمِن الخمر»(118) Paradise is forbidden for the backbiter and the habitual drunkard.

Based on narrations, whoever tracks the faults of his religious brother (and relates them to others), Allah will expose his ugliness.(119)

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: One Dirham of usury is worse than thirty-six acts of fornication, and the greatest usury is dealing with the honor of a Muslim.(120)

In a Hadith, we read: Sitting in the mosque awaiting congregational prayer is worship, provided it does not lead to backbiting.(121)

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said in his last sermon in Medina: If someone backbites, his fast is invalidated.(122) Meaning, he is deprived of the blessings and spiritual effects enjoyed by a fasting person.

If a piece is separated from a living human body, it is possible for the place to fill up, but if something is taken from a dead person, the place remains empty. Backbiting is taking away people's honor, and once honor is gone, it cannot be compensated. «لحم اخیه میتاً»

Compensation for Backbiting To compensate for backbiting committed in the past, if the person backbitten is alive; if we tell him that we have backbitten him and he becomes upset, according to some Maraji' Taqlid (123), we should not tell him, but rather repent between ourselves and Allah. If it is possible to reach the listeners of the backbiting, we should compensate for the past humiliation by speaking well of that individual and honoring him. If the person backbitten does not become upset, we should seek his forgiveness directly. However, if the person backbitten has passed away, we must repent and apologize to Allah, Who is indeed the Acceptor of repentance and the Forgiving.

Sheikh Tusi, in his commentary on Tajrid, based on a Hadith from the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, states: If the person backbitten has heard the backbiting, the compensation is to go to him, apologize, and seek his forgiveness. But if he has not heard it, we must seek forgiveness for him whenever we mention him. «انّ كفّارة الغیبة أن تستغفر لمَن اغتبتَه كلّما ذكرتَه»(124)

Cases where Backbiting is Permissible In certain cases, backbiting is permissible. We refer to some of them: 1. In the context of consultation regarding important matters; meaning if a person seeks advice about another regarding marriage, selection, or assigning responsibility, etc., we can inform the consultant of that individual's faults. 2. To refute the false speech and belief of individuals who hold such beliefs, relating their speech and the fault of their speech is permissible, lest people follow them. 3. To give testimony before a judge, the truth must be spoken, even if it constitutes backbiting. 4. To express being wronged ( مظلومیّت), stating the oppressor's injustice is permissible. 5. A person who sins shamelessly and openly; backbiting him is permissible. 6. To refute baseless claims, backbiting is permissible. If someone claims: I am a Mujtahid, a doctor, a Sayyid, and we know that he does not possess these qualities, it is permissible to inform people so they are not deceived.

Hearing Backbiting The duty of the listener is not to listen to backbiting and to defend the believer. In a Hadith, we read: «السّاكتُ شَریكُ القائل»(125) The silent person is a partner of the speaker.

The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said: Whoever hears backbiting and refutes it, Allah closes a thousand doors of evil upon him in this world and the Hereafter. But if he remains silent and listens, the sin of the speaker is also recorded for him. And if he can help the person backbitten but does not, he will be disgraced and humiliated in both worlds.(126)

In a narration, backbiting is considered kufr (disbelief), and listening to it and being pleased with it is considered shirk (polytheism).(127)

In Ayah 36 of Surah Al-Isra, we read: «انّ السّمع و البصر والفؤاد كلّ اولئك كان عنه مسئولا» Each of the human ear, eye, and heart will be questioned on the Day of Judgment. Therefore, we do not have the right to listen to every word.
